Frank L Coombs (1853-1934)
Nathan F. Coombs (1881-1972)
Frank L. Dunlap (1913-1984)
Malcolm A. Mackenzie
C. Preston Sheckelford
Randolph L Snowden
Diane M. Price
Diane L. Dillon
L. Randolph Skidmore
Charles P. Kuntz
Arthur C. Callagher

Coombs & Dunlap
Attorneys at Law
1211 Division Street
Napa, California 94559-3398
(707) 252-9100

Telecopier (707) 252-6516

St. Helena Office
1110 Adams Street
St. Helena, California 94574
(707) 963-5202

Of Counsel
June F. Moroney

February 14, 1991

File No. 15241-20006

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95814

Attention: Legal Division

City of Calistoga/Maxfield Property

Gentlemen:

I presently serve as City Attorney for the City of
Calistoga, California.

A situation has recently arisen in Calistoga
concerning which I want to seek your advice and direction.
The City has been interested for some time in acquiring land
for irrigation with treated waste water. Land for other
municipal purposes has also been under consideration.

Late last year, a committee was appointed,
consisting of members of the council and staff. At the time
the committee was selected and charged with its work, it was
known that only a few parcels of vacant land existed within
the City which might be applicable. One of those sites was
owned by a present member of Calistoga's City Council. That
member of the council did not participate in the decision to
appoint a committee or in its selection or functioning.

The committee has now made its report to the
council and has recommended that the City look into the
acquisition of the council member's property. Although I was
not City Attorney last fall when the committee was first
appointed, my predecessor warned the council of the many
problems and difficulties arising from the possible
acquisition of property from the council member. She
specifically advised them of the problems posed by statutes,
such as Government Code Section 1090. She also provided them

Fair Political Practices Commission
February 14, 1991
Page 2


with copies and a brief analysis of the California Supreme
Court case Thomson v. Call (1985), 38 Cal.3d 633, 214
Cal.Rptr. 139.

     I wrote a memorandum to the council accompanying
the committee's report. I enclose a copy of that memorandum
for your review. The councilman who owns the property,
Robert Maxfield, has acted precisely in accordance with my
recommendations.

     My questions to the Fair Political Practices
Commission are: (1) whether they are regulations,
guidelines or other printed material available to guide a
municipality through the possible acquisition of property
from a member of the city council; and (2) whether Commission
staff has any comments or additional input to supplement the
material contained in my written memorandum.

     Unless I receive some other information from you,
my reading of the applicable statutory and case law would
lead me to recommend to the council that they take the
statutory steps leading toward the institution of a
condemnation action. After that action is filed, I would
then seek an early status conference with our local Superior
Court in which I would seek to have the Court appoint a
special master to oversee negotiations leading to the City's
acquisition of the Maxfield parcel. I would direct that in
the meantime, the recommendations contained in my letter
remain in effect.

     Thank you for whatever information or assistance
you can give me. I will look forward to hearing from you.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Malcolm A. Mackenzie

106/369
Enclosure
cc: Ms. Natasha Merkuloff,
     City Administrator


                              Coombs & Dunlap

MEMORANDUM

To:    Mayor and Members of          Date:   January 18, 1991
       Calistoga City Council

From:  Malcolm A. Mackenzie          File:   No. 15241
                                             Robert Maxfield
                                             Property


Site Selection for Treated Waste Water and Community Needs

Introduction:

     The Site Selection Committee regarding treated waste water
and community needs has made a recommendation to the Mayor and
Council, dated January 16, 1991. That recommendation indicates
that the City should consider acquisition of the Robert Maxfield
property.

     That recommendation raises a number of legal problems,
because of Robert Maxfield's status as a member of the City
Council. This Memorandum will discuss those questions, make
recommendations to the Council as to how to proceed, and discuss
the legal reasoning for the recommendations.

Questions Raised:

     The questions raised by the Site Selection Committee's
recommendations include:

     (1) May the City Council discuss and act upon the
Committee's recommendations?

     (2) What restrictions and guidelines apply in any
discussion and action by the Council?

Recommendations:

     For the reasons that are explored later in this
Memorandum, I recommend that the City Council act as follows on
the Site Selection Committee's report:

     (1) The Council may discuss and act upon the Committee's
recommendation.

     (2) Council member Robert Maxfield may not participate in
the discussion, and it would be desirable if he was not physically
present in the Council chamber while the discussion took place.


                                    -1-

(3) Council member Robert Maxfield should not discuss the
Committee's recommendations with other council members or with
staff.

(4) If the Council were to determine that acquisition of
the Maxfield parcel was desirable, neither the Council nor city
staff should undertake any negotiation with Robert Maxfield
relating to the acquisition of the property.

(5) If the Council were to determine that acquisition of
the Maxfield parcel was desirable, acquisition should only take
place through the eminent domain process.

(6) The Council should direct its legal and
administrative representatives to seek guidance from the
California Fair Political Practices Commission as to the
appropriate methods of proceeding.

Discussion:

The problems raised by the acquisition of a Council
member's property by a city arise principally as a result of
Government Code Section 1090. That section reads, in pertinent
part, as follows:

"Members of the Legislature, state, county,
district, judicial district, and city officers
or employees shall not be financially interested
in any contract made by them in their official
capacity, or by any body or board of which they
are members. Nor shall state, county, district,
judicial district, and city officers or
employees be purchasers at any sale or vendors
at any purchase made by them in their official
capacity."

Section 1090 is a very direct and straightforward rule
which simply prohibits a Council member from any financial
involvement with a city. The section has been in effect for many
years and many court cases have interpreted its very strict
requirements. As courts have often pointed out, even a contract
which is fair to the city is unlawful, and the fact that full
disclosure is made is irrelevant; even abstention by the member
does not cure the problem. As the California Supreme Court
indicated in its most recent case construing Section 1090, "Mere
membership on the board or council establishes the presumption
that the officer participated in the forbidden transaction or
influenced other members of the council." (Thomson vs. Call
(1985), 38 Cal.3d 633, 649, 214 Cal.Rptr. 139)

The limitations on the city's actions are not only strict,
but the penalties for noncompliance are quite onerous. The most
recent California Supreme Court case which construes Section 1090

-2-

(Thomson v. Call, supra) involved the acquisition by the City of
Albany of some land belonging to a council member. The
acquisition was somewhat convoluted and indirect, but the trial
court determined that it was part of an overall transaction
whereby the city acquired the land from a member of the city
council. The councilman in question did not participate in most
aspects of the transaction, and made full disclosure of his
ownership of the property. The council member sought and received
and followed the advice of the city's attorney in his actions
relating to the transaction.

In spite of all of these factors and in spite of an
apparent absence of fraud and what the court called a possible
"good faith mistake on the council member's part, the court
upheld the imposition of a harsh penalty upon the council member.
That penalty was the forfeiture of the entire purchase price
received by him in the transaction.

The case which I have just described underscores the
difficulty of a city acquiring property belonging to a council
member. Such acquisition remains possible, however. Some hints
as to how to proceed are contained in a 1988 California Court of
Appeal decision (Santa Clara Valley Water District v. Gross
(1988), 200 Cal.App.3d 1363, 246 Cal.Rptr. 580). In that case, a
water district sought to acquire property belonging to a member of
its board of directors, and the specific issue before the court
involved whether certain rules in eminent domain litigation
applied in light of Government Code Section 1090. The court held
that the rules did apply, and seemed to suggest that when an
eminent domain action is filed, that some of the requirements of
Section 1090 may not apply. Two caveats should be noted, however:
first, it is clear that Council member Maxfield should not
negotiate with other members of the Council or staff; and, second,
the FPPC may not view the matter in the same manner as the Court
of Appeal.

It is for this reason that I advocate seeking the FPPC's
advice.

Conclusion.

If the Council determines that acquisition should be
further explored, steps toward eminent domain should be
undertaken, and the FPPC's advice should be sought.

M.A.M.

106/369
91.1
BY FACSIMILE
